coredata.dc:title: Highly improved mechanical performances of polyvinyl butyral through fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Chao
Li, Ning
Xue, Chaorui
Wang, Huiqi
Liu, Hantao
Chang, Qing
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: A new application of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with low cost and environmental friendliness is developed by incorporating them into polyvinyl butyral (PVB) polymer matrix. The introduction of CDs significantly enhances the mechanical properties of PVB. The yield strength, tensile strength, and adhesion strength are 200%, 144% and 13-fold higher than those of pure PVB. Ultra-small size and abundant O-related groups endows CDs with superior affinity and strong binding force with PVB molecules, giving rise to the remarkable enhancement of PVB mechanical properties. Therefore, this work offers a promising substitute for PVB as interlayer materials of the automotive windshield.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Polyvinyl butyral
Nanocomposites
Properties
Reinforcing mechanisms

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs (Inset: HRTEM image of a nanoparticle) and (b) their corresponding size distribution; (c) PL spectra of CD with different concentrations excited at the same wavelength; (d) PL spectra from the solid films of pure PVB and CD-embedded PVB.
(a) Stress–strain curves, (b) Storage modulus, (c) Degree of entanglement values and (d) Adhesion strength of F1-F4.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) TGA curves of the samples; Mechanism schematic of (c) pure PVB and (d) CD-embedded PVB.

head.ce:title: Highly improved mechanical performances of polyvinyl butyral through fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new application of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with low cost and environmental friendliness is developed by incorporating them into polyvinyl butyral (PVB) polymer matrix. The introduction of CDs significantly enhances the mechanical properties of PVB. The yield strength, tensile strength, and adhesion strength are 200%, 144% and 13-fold higher than those of pure PVB. Ultra-small size and abundant O-related groups endows CDs with superior affinity and strong binding force with PVB molecules, giving rise to the remarkable enhancement of PVB mechanical properties. Therefore, this work offers a promising substitute for PVB as interlayer materials of the automotive windshield.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyvinyl butyral (PVB), as a commercially important thermoplastic polymer, can be used in many cases that require strong binding, optical clarity, adhesion to many surfaces, toughness and flexibility. One of the most important applications of PVB polymer is to be served as an interlayer material of automotive windshield. To obtain advanced polymer composites with superior properties, incorporating nanoparticles has been proposed and demonstrated to be a highly effective and low-cost approach [1]. Till now, PVB nanocomposites, reinforced by TiO2 particles, carbon nanotubes, inorganic clay, and graphene, have been reported [2–6]. However, these fillers have larger sizes than single PVB molecule, only leading to limited improvement in macroscopic properties. Reducing nanoparticle size could increase the interfacial layer volume fraction at fixed loading and offer a potential to enhance desirable properties [7]. But the weak interactions between polymer and nanoparticle would result in aggregation and poor dispersion [1], which is unfavorable for improving properties.

Carbon dots (CDs), which are discrete quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm and have outstanding fluorescence properties [8], are of interest in nanocomposites because they are readily synthesized, low cost, and good dispersibility in many solvent [9]. Besides, the surfaces of CDs are covered by oxygen-containing groups (5–50 wt%), which make them easy to interact with polymer molecules so as to improve the interfacial bonding. Therefore, CDs could be expected to be effective nanofillers to enhance the properties of PVB.

Herein, we synthesized less than 5 nm of CDs by selective oxidation etching of cheap charcoal with the mixed solution of formic acid and hydrogen peroxide [10], then directly transferred as-prepared CDs into PVB ethanol solution to achieve a new CDs-embedded PVB nanocomposite after removing solvent. The performances of nanocomposite film were evaluated and the underlying mechanisms of the effects of CDs were discussed.

TEM image of as-prepared CDs (Fig. 1 a) reveals that CDs with size about 3.1 nm (Fig. 1b) are uniformly dispersed. The carbon nanoparticle has well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 1a), corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene [10]. Fig. 1c and S1 show that the photoluminescent (PL) properties of as-prepared CDs are dependent on their concentrations. Increasing concentration leads to red-shift of PL peak and decrease of PL intensity. Such phenomenon could originate from energy transfer between CDs or self-absorption at high concentration [11]. The red-shift of the optical absorption (Fig. S1b) and shorter decay lifetime of CDs (Fig. S2) at high concentration also confirm the energy transfer between CDs [12]. These results demonstrate that the interactions between CDs have important impact on their PL properties.

Fig. 1d shows the PL spectra from CD-embedded PVB nanocomposites containing 0, 0.08, 0.16, 0.24 wt% of CDs, named as F1-F4 respectively. While pure PVB film can’t emit fluorescence, CD-embedded PVB film exhibit fluorescence and the PL intensities decrease slightly with increasing the content of CDs (Fig. 1d, Figs. S3 and S4). More intriguingly, different from CDs solution, the PL peaks of the nanocomposites have almost no changes while varying the contents of CDs or excitation wavelengths, suggesting that the radiative recombination from direct energy transfer between CDs is hindered. This demonstrates that CDs and PVB molecules have strong interaction that makes PVB attach to the surface of CDs (similar to surface passivation) [13], thus the CDs in PVB matrix exhibit their intrinsic luminescence of an isolated nanoparticle. The effect of the passivation of PVB is further verified by the result that the lifetime of CDs-incorporated PVB film becomes longer than that of CDs with high concentration and shows fewer differences with variation of CDs-loading concentration (Fig. S5). Moreover, the incorporation of CDs also heightens the ultraviolet light absorption of PVB film (Fig. S6), giving rise to their promise use for UV protection.

Fig. 2 a shows the stress–strain curves of F1-F4. Excitingly, CD-embedded PVB nanocomposite films exhibit much better mechanical properties than pure PVB. The yield strength and tensile strength of the composite film with 0.16 wt% of CDs reach the maximum value of 51.68 and 36.9 MPa, respectively, about 200 and 144% higher than those of pure PVB. Further increase of the content of CDs, the yield strength and tensile strength decrease but the fracture strain keeps increasing. To understand the great effect of CDs in improving the mechanical properties of CD-embedded PVB films, DMA was performed for exploring the interaction between CDs and PVB chains. Fig. 2b shows the variation of storage modulus (E) with temperature of the samples. The nanocomposite films present much higher storage modules than pure PVB at room temperature. Since the glassy region is dominated by the action of the intermolecular force which is dependent on the entanglement of PVB chains, we further calculated the degree of entanglement (N) (Fig. 2c) and the effectiveness of CDs as reinforcing phase (C coefficient, Fig. S7) according to DMA results (See supporting information) [14]. Compared to pure PVB, CD-embedded PVB films exhibit higher N values and C coefficients, strongly proving the higher interfacial interactions between PVB and CDs [15]. To gain better insight into the interaction, XPS, FTIR and TGA measurements (Fig. S8, Fig. 3 a and b) were performed. It is found that no new covalent bond or peak exist for CD-embedded PVB, indicating that the strong interaction between PVB molecules and the surface groups of CDs is formed through hydrogen bonds or intermolecular forces.

Based on the above results, the reinforcing mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 3c and d. For pure PVB, the intermolecular forces are only caused by the interaction between PVB molecules. When pure PVB film is subjected to external forces (Fig. 3c), the internal defects appear and gradually expand through breaking the intermolecular interaction between PVB chains. After the introduction of CDs, the strong interaction between PVB molecules and CDs appear and create “bridging interactions” between PVB chains mediated by CDs [2]. Therefore, the internal crack can be impeded as it propagates to the surface of CDs (Fig. 3d). Only when increasing the load, PVB molecular chain can be stripped from the surface of the CDs, thus giving rise to higher strength and larger elongation at fracture [16]. However, the exorbitant content of CDs could weaken intermolecular forces between PVB molecules due to the strong attractive PVB-CD interaction and/or“microphase separation”, resulting in the decrease of matrix strength (Fig. 2).

Adhesion strength, another critical factor for PVB when used as interlayer materials, was further studied by lap shear measurement (Fig. S9) [17]. The adhesion strength of nanocomposite film containing 0.16 wt% CDs reaches the maximum value and is approximately thirteen times higher than that of pure PVB (Fig. 2d). Besides the interaction between CDs and PVB, the improved adhesion strength is also related to the increased oxygen-containing groups from CDs, which interact with the active sites on the glass substrate surface (such as Si–O–Si or Si–O–H groups) [3] and lead to the formation of lots of hydrogen-bond, therefore further strengthening the adhesion force. The higher adhesion strength is desirable for CD-embedded PVB nanocomposites as the interlayer material in the laminated safety glass.

Furthermore, adhesion factor (A) which represents the degree of interaction or adhesion between the phases is studied (Fig. S10). The A value of CD-embedded PVB nanocomposite is much greater than that of the composites of graphene oxide and PVB [6], strongly suggesting that the ultra-small CDs as reinforcements are more suitable than high-dimensional nanomaterials. Moreover, the incorporation of CDs can also make the PVB film possess better thermal insulation performance (See supporting information, Fig. S11).

We developed a new approach of incorporating CDs into the PVB network via an easy and low-cost process. The resulting nanocomposites not only exhibited superior mechanical properties, but also possessed a good thermal insulation property. The significantly enhanced performances of CD-incorporated PVB could be mainly attributed to the interaction of CDs with PVB molecules. This work opens up a new utilization way of fluorescent CDs.

Chao Gao: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. Ning Li: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Chaorui Xue: Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Huiqi Wang: Resources, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Hantao Liu: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Qing Chang: Writing - original draft, Validation, Formal analysis. Jinlong Yang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Shengliang Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125, 51502270, 21703209), Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi, Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082), Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.128537.
